title: Open Workshop: Scaling as a Solo Builder, Tariffs, Employee Mistakes, and Design Budget Misalignment
summary: Erin hosted an open Q&A session covering a range of real challenges faced by builders at various stages. Topics included scaling from a one-person operation, managing mock-up expectations with subcontractors, navigating tariff increases, handling employee errors and bonus structures, and addressing misalignment between interior design concepts and client budgets.

teachings.concept: Setting Expectations with Subcontractors on Mock-Ups
teachings.explanation: The builder must establish upfront that mock-ups are a standard requirement and not a billable extra. Getting the subcontractor's raw price first, then having the conversation about mock-up expectations, prevents price inflation and sets a professional tone for the working relationship.
teachings.quote: The builder needs to set the tone for that is the expectation because you're looking out for them because you want them to execute something one time.

teachings.concept: Scalable Templates: Amplified vs. Simplified Use
teachings.explanation: Systems and templates built for larger teams can still serve solo operators. A one-person shop can use only the relevant columns or sections, running through the whole system personally rather than delegating, without needing to build out the full infrastructure before it is useful.
teachings.quote: You may just want to fill out two columns of it, right? Cause you have stuff, you have everything in your head and you may not want to spend a bunch of time.

teachings.explanation: Adding a confidence percentage to each line item on a budget sheet — asking whether a number is 100% confirmed and why if not — is a differentiating practice that positions the builder as a trustworthy teammate to architects and designers, and prevents surprises down the line.
teachings.quote: I don't know any builders in the industry doing that. There probably are a few, but like, that is a major secret sauce.

teachings.concept: Separating People from Problems in Cost Disputes
teachings.explanation: When tariffs or unforeseen cost increases arise, builders should separate the human emotional response from the factual problem. The problem is simply that a price went up. The builder's obligation is to communicate the facts clearly, not to absorb costs out of guilt or anxiety.
teachings.quote: What I owe my client is a conversation. That's the problem. The, when we start thinking about the people, oh, I feel terrible... those need to be separate.

teachings.concept: Tariff Contingency as a Line Item
teachings.explanation: Rather than scrambling when material costs spike due to tariffs, builders can proactively add a tariff contingency line item to project budgets, similar to how banks required a 10% contingency during COVID. This prepares homeowners and protects the builder without fabricating numbers.
teachings.quote: A smart move could be, you know, maybe we have a tariff contingency line item at X percentage. And that's there if we need it right in order to prep our homeowners.

teachings.concept: Demanding Proof Before Paying Tariff Increases
teachings.explanation: When vendors present large, fear-driven cost increases due to tariffs, builders should push back and require actual documentation of the cost before agreeing to pass it along. Initial inflated estimates often shrink significantly once real receipts are produced.
teachings.quote: It's a fear charge. And so you're right. Like, again, it's not part of the equation, but we're happy to pay for it if it is part of the equation.

teachings.concept: Coaching Employee Mistakes Through Questions, Not Accusations
teachings.explanation: When team members make costly errors, starting with open questions — asking what happened and why — gives employees the chance to reflect and self-correct. This avoids the dynamic of the boss as the heavy and allows people to take ownership more naturally.
teachings.quote: By opening that conversation up, you're giving them a chance to reflect really on what they're doing and how they can improve.

teachings.concept: Micro Moments as a Budget Communication Tool
teachings.explanation: When explaining why a design is over budget, breaking down individual design choices into specific cost comparisons — such as comparing wood cladding versus drywall on a single wall — helps clients and designers understand how small decisions compound into large budget overruns without the builder appearing as the naysayer.
teachings.quote: All of these micro moments in your home are a math equation. And so I just want to bring up that equation right now, because we may be looking at a hundred of them.

teachings.concept: You Cannot Grow What You Do Not Know
teachings.explanation: A general contractor cannot effectively delegate or grow a company without first developing a trained eye for quality and process on the job site. Knowing the craft deeply enough to identify errors is the foundation for earning reputation and trust, which is what actually drives company growth.
teachings.quote: You will never grow if you don't know your shit. That's how you grow.

actionable_advice: Get a subcontractor's raw price first before discussing mock-up requirements, then have the conversation about expectations so they cannot inflate pricing in response.

Use the amplified templates in a simplified way as a solo operator — fill in only the relevant columns and run the whole system yourself with a regular time block, such as two hours on Tuesday morning to call all trades.
Add a tariff contingency line item to project budgets rather than absorbing unexpected cost increases or leaving homeowners unprepared.
Place orders for appliances and materials now to avoid future tariff-driven price increases, while being aware that warranty periods may begin at ship date.
When a vendor presents a large tariff-based increase, do not pay it without requiring actual documentation of the cost; insist on seeing the real receipt before agreeing to any amount.
When tariff or market cost increases hit, call every client proactively and ask what they want to do — give them the agency to decide rather than making assumptions.
When addressing employee mistakes tied to a bonus structure, start the conversation by asking what happened and why before discussing money or consequences.
Before presenting budget numbers to clients, meet with the full design and architecture team first — three parties together — to surface misalignments before they become client-facing problems.
When design concepts are clearly over budget, prepare a preliminary spreadsheet with specific line-item comparisons so you are not perceived as the naysayer without data.
If you are a general contractor who cannot be on site full time, hire a superintendent or someone with deep field knowledge to fill that gap — do not attempt to grow without that coverage in place.
client_challenges: Solo builder struggling with when and how to hire first employees and how to onboard subcontractors to standards like mock-ups without losing them or inflating costs.
Builder in Canada navigating 25% tariffs on aluminum and fabricated materials from the US, with vendors pushing cost increases onto them and clients resisting absorbing those costs.

High-end remodeler whose field crew made multiple costly errors — denting a front door and a freezer — causing a significant drop in the quarterly bonus pool and creating a difficult conversation about accountability.

Builder dealing with an interior design firm whose renderings are dramatically over the homeowner's confirmed budget, with no material specs provided, and concern about being perceived as the obstacle rather than a professional advocate.

New contractor who failed a shear wall inspection because an incorrect nail pattern was missed, and is questioning how to structure oversight and whether she can grow the company without being deeply technical herself.
Builder evaluating whether to stay in renovation or move into new construction, given strong local market growth between Omaha and Lincoln, Nebraska, and pressure from trades to scale up.
notable_quotes: On mock-up culture: 'Most of the trades will automatically make mock-ups now because they don't want to redo things... they actually love making mock-ups now. It's just that whole mindset needs to change.'
On budget confidence factors: 'I don't know any builders in the industry doing that. There probably are a few, but like, that is a major secret sauce.'

On tariff panic charges: 'It would be like me turning a contract into a client and putting a line item for fear and then plugging in a hundred thousand dollars. That's what vendors are doing to builders.'
On separating emotion from cost disputes: 'What I owe my client is a conversation. That's the problem.'
On growing a construction company: 'You will never grow if you don't know your shit. That's how you grow.'
On micro-moment budgeting: 'All of these micro moments in your home are a math equation. And so I just want to bring up that equation right now, because we may be looking at a hundred of them.'
